Codex even folds in the substantive carry-forwards (bounded merge-insertion, skip/too-close-to-call as first-class, offline-safe metadata default, non-blocking paywall) rather than treating them as still up for debate. No one is contesting the routing-header reading anymore — both speakers cite the same three prompt fields as dispositive.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

This is a portfolio **child** project, not a portfolio parent — the routing header (`PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true`, `Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7`, `Selected app slug: faveoff`) means the cross-project selection (10 candidates → best 5) already happened one level up, before this child folder was ever created. That work is not this phase's job to redo, and doing so would have produced a manifest with four unrelated sibling apps (`mealsprint`, `loopfit`, `snapwardrobe`, `walletriff`) marked `build: true` inside the FaveOff folder — which doesn't belong here and risks real confusion downstream if anything acted on it.

**Decision for this phase:** No portfolio split is needed at this level. This child project has exactly one selected app:

```json
{
  "portfolio_parent": "multi-app-exp7",
  "portfolio_child": true,
  "selected_apps": [
    {
      "slug": "faveoff",
      "category": "Entertainment / Taste Ranking",
      "promise": "Rank watched movies and shows head-to-head to build a shareable personal top 10 taste card.",
      "target_user": "Casual watchers who find star ratings meaningless and want a fast, decisive, shareable read on their own taste.",
      "build": true
    }
  ]
}
```

Everything already locked in `prompt_contract` and `product_research` (bounded merge-insertion ranking, offline-first metadata provider as default with a "skip/too-close-to-call" escape that doesn't break the sort invariant, track-level movies-free/TV-paid gate, non-blocking sandbox-only StoreKit paywall, widget lowest-priority) carries forward unchanged — this phase doesn't reopen any of it. The one thing both speakers want carried into the build phases as a live constraint, not just a nice-to-have: the test for scope creep is whether a first-time user can go from zero titles to a shareable top-10 card in a couple of minutes without hitting a screen that requires reading instructions first — any core-loop screen that fails that is a defect, not a polish item.
phase_outputs.initial_discussion:
CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Codex and Claude are lined up here — same promise, same user, same loop, and Claude's additions fill in gaps rather than fight anything Codex said.

**Product promise:** FaveOff turns quick head-to-head picks between watched titles into a real, trustworthy personal top 10 — something you'd actually be confident sharing, not a star rating you don't believe.

**Primary user & scenario:** A casual movie/TV watcher who has opinions but no system for them — not a completionist, not someone building an archive. The scenario that matters most isn't a big one-time onboarding dump; it's the steady-state habit of "I watched something, let me place it" — add a couple of titles a week, get a couple of quick comparisons, watch the list update. That recurring small loop is what the app needs to nail, not a one-time gauntlet.

**Core loop:** Add a title (manual entry by default, offline-safe placeholder art) → Compare it against the ranked list via bounded binary-insertion match-ups (with a skip/"haven't thought about it" escape) → **Placed** — a distinct, small payoff moment showing where it landed ("#4 of 12, ahead of X, behind Y") that both agree is the actual reward beat and can't be skipped over silently → Browse the living ranked list, filterable by genre/decade → Share a Taste Card via ImageRenderer + ShareLink. When there's nothing left to compare, the app shows a calm steady-state ("add something to keep ranking") rather than manufacturing forced re-comparisons just to stay engaging.

**Hard scope boundaries:** No accounts, no social graph, no network-first architecture, no music/album track in v1, no ML in the ranking loop, no full round-robin (n²) comparisons. Movies are the free-ranked track; TV titles can be added/logged for free but only ranked after upgrading (carried over from earlier phases). No manual drag-to-reorder of the list — the whole point is that comparisons, not curation, produce the order. No tutorial/instruction screens — if the comparison screen's two-tap interaction isn't self-evident on its own, that's a design defect to fix, not something to patch with an explainer.

**Measurable success criteria:** A brand-new user should reach a shareable card (even a small one, 3–5 titles) in a few minutes of real interaction, not a big session; ranking must be stable — the same two titles should never resolve to a contradictory order later; and the spec's own "abandons after a handful of comparisons" failure mode gets an operational definition: if most first-session users don't complete at least 5 comparisons, that's the tedious-flow failure, and the fix is a smaller default list or faster convergence — not more onboarding. Offline behavior (placeholder art, working share/export with no network) and persistence across relaunch are part of the bar, not stretch goals.

**Open tension flagged, not a blocker:** Claude names its own risk here — betting that "no tutorial, no manual reorder" holds up once real screens exist. If a later phase's wireframes show people genuinely lost on first launch, that's worth revisiting rather than defending on principle.
phase_outputs.per_app_product_brief:
Both speakers landed in the same place again — this is the fourth phase in a row where Codex lays out the framework and Claude locks specific numbers/decisions on top of it without contradicting anything. No open fight here.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user & use case:** Someone who watches 2-3 things a week, has a strong reaction the moment the credits roll, but has never been able to confidently answer "what's actually your favorite movie." Not a Letterboxd person, not a completionist — they'll bounce off anything that smells like inventory management or logging. The use case is narrow and recurring: place the thing I just watched relative to what I already know I liked, and eventually have something worth showing off. Working App Store subtitle: "You already know which one was better."

**Paid value & subscription value:** Free tier is a genuinely complete, satisfying product — unlimited movie adds, full movie ranking via the comparison engine, genre/decade filtering, and full Taste Card generation + sharing for the movies track, capped at 25 ranked movies (large enough for a real top 10 to surface repeatedly, small enough to keep re-rank fast and leave paid list-length removal meaningful). A crippled free tier would kill the viral loop this app depends on, so the cap is generous by design, not a teaser. FaveOff+ ($3.99/mo or $24.99/yr) unlocks real functional depth, not cosmetics: the TV ranking track (TV titles can already be logged for free per the track-level gate; ranking and TV Taste Cards are paid), removal of the 25-item cap, saved/nameable genre-decade sub-ranking views instead of one-off filters, and periodic recap cards ("Your Q3 Rewatch") reusing the same Taste Card renderer against a different data slice.

**Core loop:** Watch something → add it in under 10 seconds (title + type, offline placeholder art by default) → one comparison at a time against the existing ranked list via bounded binary-insertion (skip/"too close to call" escape available) → the **Placed** moment as a first-class, purpose-built screen state — not a derived view of the list — showing rank number, what it beat, what beat it → back to browsing, or continue if insertion isn't done. Bounded at log2(n) comparisons per insert, never more. Cold-start/edge case locked now: if a title is auto-placed without a comparison (empty list or single-item list), the Placed copy must be coherent ("first one in — add another to start ranking"), not a broken "#1 of 1, ahead of nothing" string.

**Competitive wedge:** Letterboxd/IMDb/Rotten Tomatoes assume you already know your opinion and ask you to type it as a number or review — recall. FaveOff produces the opinion by making you choose — recognition, the same reason personality quizzes finish and forms don't. Against Beli (the closest real analog), the wedge is that posters carry identity weight restaurant photos never will, so the Taste Card has more emotional pull as a shareable object. Against bracket/tournament apps, the wedge is persistence — this is a living list that grows with you, not a one-shot bracket that resets, which is why someone opens it a second and fiftieth time.

**Viral/growth angle:** The Taste Card is the growth loop, not a leaderboard or social graph. The trigger is disagreement — "no way you have [movie] above [movie]" — and the natural reply is "make your own and prove me wrong," a zero-friction acquisition loop with no account creation or friend-finding required. Concrete design requirement flowing out of this: the card needs a small persistent brand mark and must read clearly at iMessage/Instagram-story thumbnail size, not just full-screen — if it looks generic or unbranded, the share doesn't convert to installs.

**Local-first & cloud-ready plan:** SwiftData is the sole source of truth for titles, comparisons, and computed rank position — no server round-trip anywhere in the core loop. Poster/metadata network lookup sits behind a protocol with an offline-first stub (manual entry, initials/color-block art) as the default happy path; missing metadata means "compare anyway, enrich later," never "cannot proceed." Cloud-readiness is an architectural bet enforced by code review, not a built feature: no view or ranking code may reach into SwiftData directly — everything routes through a repository protocol, so a future CloudKit sync layer or account system can slot in later without touching ranking logic.
phase_outputs.next_steps_small:
CONSENSUS: YES

They actually landed on a real compromise here, not just Claude caving. Claude looked back at round 1 and admitted lumping filters and the cap in with the paywall was wrong — once genre/decade are already fields on `Title` (which both agreed to two rounds ago), filter chips are just a `.filter{}` and an empty-state, not new scope, and cutting them would mean only testing four of the five locked core workflows. Same logic for the 25-item cap: it's a count check plus one static "you're capped" screen, and skipping it would quietly decide "free ranking is unlimited" without anyone actually deciding that. Codex gets both of those.

On the actual StoreKit purchase flow, Codex came around too, once Claude answered the real worry directly: the thing that prevents "hidden churn" (someone hitting a wall and discovering rules that weren't there before) is the boundary being visible and consistent — TV shows as ranking-disabled, the cap fires at 25 — not whether the upgrade button completes a real transaction. Both now agree the upgrade CTA is real, tappable UI, but it opens a "coming soon" sheet in this slice rather than wiring actual StoreKit 2 purchases, sandbox config, and restore logic — that's explicitly named as the next slice, not a cut feature.

## Final Output

**MVP slice for this phase:** one continuous, offline-first movies-centered loop — add a title (title + type, optional genre/decade, placeholder art, no network dependency) → bounded binary-insertion comparison with a skip/"too close to call" escape → a real Placed confirmation screen (rank + neighbors, with coherent 0/1-item cold-start copy) → ranked list view with genre/decade filter chips → Taste Card generation via `ImageRenderer` + `ShareLink`. Everything persists across relaunch through SwiftData and works with the network off.

**Must-have interactions:**
- Manual add-title form (title, type toggle, optional genre/decade — never required to proceed)
- Compare screen: two-way choice + skip, with deterministic, sort-order-preserving insertion (~log₂n per insert)
- Placed confirmation as its own designed state, not a derived list view
- Ranked list with genre/decade filter chips (minimal — filter-and-empty-state only, no saved views)
- TV titles are addable but show as ranking-disabled with a real, tappable "Unlock with FaveOff+" CTA that opens a "coming soon" sheet (no live transaction)
- Free movie-ranking cap enforced at 25, with its own capped-state screen using the same CTA/sheet pattern
- Taste Card render + working ShareLink export
- Full data model locked now: `Title.type`, optional genre, optional decade/year — so no future migration is needed even though filter UI and StoreKit are sequenced later
- Empty/loading/success/error states and VoiceOver labels on every reachable screen in this slice

**Cut list:** real StoreKit 2 purchase flow (sandbox config, transactions, restore-purchase) — explicitly the next slice, not deferred indefinitely; no tutorial/onboarding; no WidgetKit; no periodic recap cards; no saved/named sub-rank profiles; no drag-to-reorder; no live metadata/poster API in the core loop.

**Validation criteria:** fresh install, no network — add 3+ movies, complete bounded comparisons, reach a coherent ranked list and a legible, shareable Taste Card in under ~3 minutes; the same two titles compared twice never produce a contradictory order, including after relaunch; skip never corrupts the sort invariant; adding a TV title never pollutes movie ranking and never blocks the movie loop; the cap state appears predictably at exactly 25 ranked movies without blocking further adds; every screen in this slice shows correct empty/loading/success/error behavior. Failure on any of these is a defect in this slice, not a note for later polish.
phase_outputs.detailed_discussion:
CONSENSUS: YES

Codex came back and conceded both of the open points cleanly rather than re-arguing them: pending/insertion-state persistence is locked in as required now (not deferred), and the real sandboxed StoreKit 2 flow is locked into this build rather than staying a "coming soon" stub. Claude's round 3 message lands in the same place on both — it refines the pending-state point (agreeing crash-recovery persistence is required, but conceding that a visible "queue depth" UI for deliberate rapid-adding can genuinely wait as a post-v1 enhancement, since those turned out to be separable asks) and doesn't need to push further on StoreKit since Codex already got there first. The one new thing Claude adds — a named worst-case test scenario (skip the first two comparisons on a large list, fall back to the bottom with honest "come back and compare it for a more precise spot" copy instead of a vague "quick-pass" label) — is additive, not contested. Nothing left unresolved that changes build scope.

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements**
- Ranking engine is plain **binary-search insertion** into an already-sorted list (≤ ceil(log2(n+1)) real comparisons per insert). The "Ford-Johnson" name is retired from all downstream docs/code comments — it described the wrong algorithm.
- **Skip contract**: each insertion tracks a search window (`lo`, `hi`), `lastComparedIndex`, and a 2-skip uncertainty budget. Two skips in a row without a decisive pick terminates the insertion and places the title at the nearest edge of the current window (or `lo` if no real comparison ever happened). This must never contradict a previously confirmed ordering.
- Placement copy must be honest about how a title landed: a title placed via full comparisons gets normal "#N, ahead of X, behind Y" copy; a title placed via the skip-fallback with zero real comparisons gets explicit, non-generic copy (e.g. "added to the bottom of your list — come back and compare it for a more precise spot"), not a vague "quick-pass" label.
- Schema locks now: `Title.type` (movie/TV), `Title.year: Int?` (decade derived as `(year/10)*10`, never stored as free text), `Title.genre` as a single-pick fixed enum (not free text, not multi-select), and `Title.rankingState` (`unranked` / `insertionInProgress` / `ranked`) with the active insertion's search bounds (`lo`, `hi`, `lastComparedIndex`, `skipBudget`) persisted in SwiftData — required for crash/interruption recovery, not deferred. A full rapid-add "N more to place" queue UI is explicitly deferred post-v1; the persisted state just has to make resuming a single in-flight insertion safe.
- **Real StoreKit 2 purchase flow ships in this build**, sandbox-only: local `.storekit` config with both subscription products, a real upgrade sheet (price, buy state, pending/failed states), `Transaction.currentEntitlements` reconciliation on launch, a visible Restore Purchases action, and purchase failures fully contained to their own sheet so they can never block or stall the ranking loop. This reverses nothing — it fulfills what `prompt_contract` already locked as part of "production-ready"; the earlier "coming soon" sheet was a checkpoint for the smaller `next_steps_small` slice, not the final answer.
- Deletion: removing a title soft-archives/ignores referencing comparisons (no dangling pointers, no crash); deleting a title mid-active-comparison cancels that insertion and returns to the list with explicit "title removed, comparison canceled" messaging; a title baked into an already-generated Taste Card is safe because `ImageRenderer` output is a frozen bitmap, not a live reference.
- Duplicates are allowed by design (no uniqueness constraint, no merge-by-title) with an optional, non-blocking "similar title already exists" nudge.
- Subscription lapse: never hide or delete data a user already generated; only new paid-tier ranking actions (TV ranking, beyond-cap movie ranking) are blocked until re-entitlement.
- Accessibility contrast is a concrete, testable gate, not an adjective: marquee-red-on-near-black must hit **≥4.5:1 for body-weight text and ≥3:1 for large/bold text (WCAG AA)**, verified across the Dynamic Type range, before any comparison or Placed screen is considered done.
- Non-goals locked explicitly for this build: no push notifications, no custom Photos-save button (rely on ShareLink's built-in save), no data export/backup beyond the generated Taste Card image, no widget in this slice, no social graph, no forced re-comparisons, no manual drag-to-reorder, no tutorial/onboarding screens.

**Edge cases**
- 0/1-title cold starts produce coherent, non-broken Placed copy ("first one in — add another to start ranking"), never a nonsensical "#1 of 1" string.
- Skip-twice-immediately on a large (near-25-item) list is a named test case: must terminate deterministically, fall back to the window edge, and show honest bottom-placement copy rather than reading like a bug.
- App killed/backgrounded mid-insertion: resumed from persisted `lo`/`hi`/`lastComparedIndex` state, never silently loses the title or drops it into the ranked list without having gone through comparison or an honest skip-fallback.
- Title deleted while actively displayed in a live comparison screen: cancel cleanly, return to list, no crash.
- Duplicate titles (including legitimately same-named different works) are allowed and ranked independently; disambiguated by year when present.
- TV title added when movie list is at the 25-item cap: TV add still succeeds (TV was never counted against the movie cap), remains addable/loggable, stays visibly ranking-disabled with the "Unlock with FaveOff+" CTA.
- Filter chips with no matching results show a nudge state, not a blank screen.
- Purchase flow: slow/failed network mid-checkout stays fully contained to the purchase sheet; ranking loop keeps working unaffected the entire time.

**Data and privacy implications**
- No accounts, no cross-device sync, no analytics requirement this phase, no camera/mic/location permissions, no personal identifiers beyond user-entered text.
- All data lives locally in SwiftData; uninstalling the app deletes it via OS defaults — named openly as an accepted v1 limitation (no export/backup beyond the Taste Card image), with CloudKit sync as the stated future answer, not built now.
- Metadata network lookup (if ever wired) stays opt-in, behind a protocol, with the offline stub as the default happy path — never load-bearing for the core loop.
- StoreKit is the one real network touchpoint in this build; it's isolated so its failure modes can't leak into ranking/offline behavior.

**Risk register**
- *Trust collapse from arbitrary-feeling placements* → mitigated by bounded insertion + deterministic skip rule + honest, scenario-specific placement copy (not generic "quick-pass" language) for the low-confidence fallback case.
- *Crash/interruption data loss or invariant corruption* → mitigated by persisting rankingState + active insertion search bounds in SwiftData; resuming from persisted state rather than in-memory view state.
- *Condensed cinematic typography failing contrast at accessibility sizes* → mitigated by the explicit 4.5:1 / 3:1 WCAG AA targets checked across the Dynamic Type range before any screen ships.
- *Silent scope drift on StoreKit* → closed this phase by explicitly locking the real sandboxed flow into the build and naming it as not on the "safe to cut under time pressure" list (that list stays: widget first, then design polish — never ranking engine, comparison loop, offline behavior, share flow, or now StoreKit).
- *First-launch confusion with no tutorial* → stays a named, accepted bet, with an explicit bailout path recorded: if build/test reveals real confusion, the fix is simplifying the comparison screen's own affordances (larger VS-style divider, obvious two-tap zones, a first-time-only inline hint in empty-state copy), not adding a separate onboarding flow — that distinction is preserved so a later phase can't casually undo it.
- *Uninstall = total data loss* → named and accepted as a v1 limitation, deferred to the future CloudKit roadmap rather than solved now.
- *Rank position writes are O(n) per insert* → named as an explicit, accepted assumption; trivial at the 25-item free cap and still trivial at realistic paid-tier list sizes, not something requiring a different data structure in v1.

**Final assumptions**
- This is a single-app child build (FaveOff only); no further portfolio split applies here.
- Movies are the only free-ranked track; TV is addable/loggable for free but ranking-disabled until upgrade.
- Free movie ranking is capped at exactly 25 titles, enforced with its own capped-state screen.
- The comparison/skip/placement mechanics described above are the actual algorithm to build — not classic Elo, not full Ford-Johnson merge-insertion, not full round-robin.
- Every reachable screen carries empty/loading/success/error states, VoiceOver labels, and 44pt touch targets as a hard requirement, not polish.
- Widget is the only feature still explicitly first-to-cut under time pressure; everything else locked in this phase (including the real StoreKit flow) is now a hard build requirement for v1.
phase_outputs.app_features:
CONSENSUS: YES

They've closed both gaps the last round left open. Codex flipped accessibility (contrast ratios, VoiceOver, 44pt targets) from Should to Must, agreeing with Claude that this was already locked with real numbers in an earlier phase and can't be treated as a later hardening pass. On deletion, Codex wanted it as its own Must bullet; Claude's counter — fold delete-safety (no crash, no corruption, clean cancellation) into the crash-safe-persistence must-have's acceptance criteria since they're really the same "what happens when the thing my insertion referenced disappears" problem, while keeping "where the delete button actually lives in the UI" as a separate Should — is a packaging difference, not a substance disagreement; both agree deletion must never crash or corrupt state, they just organize it differently. Nothing else moved: the core loop, the insertion engine, the cap/TV boundary, StoreKit, and the Taste Card are Must for both, and the won't-build list hasn't been touched by either side in two rounds. Claude's added "count the taps" test for whether policy screens (TV lock, cap, paywall) ever appear unprompted is a concrete tightening of a risk Codex already named, not a new fight.

## Final Output

**Must-have features** (each with its user story and falsifiable acceptance test):

1. **Add a title** — as a user I can add a movie or TV title with just name + type in under 10 seconds, optional year/genre, no network required. *Acceptance:* with airplane mode on, adding a title succeeds, shows placeholder initials/color-block art, and appears in the correct unranked/pending state immediately.

2. **Head-to-head comparison with skip** — as a user placing a new title I see one pairwise choice at a time, bounded to `ceil(log2(n+1))` comparisons, with a skip option that terminates deterministically after two consecutive skips. *Acceptance:* adding a 25th title and skipping the first two comparisons lands it at the bottom with the exact non-generic copy ("added to the bottom for now — compare later to refine"), never a vague "quick-pass" label, and never contradicts a previously confirmed pairwise result.

3. **Placed confirmation screen** — as a user I see exactly where my title landed and what it's adjacent to, as a designed reward moment, not a passive list refresh. *Acceptance:* 0-item and 1-item cold starts produce coherent copy ("first one in..."), never a broken "#1 of 1, ahead of nothing" string.

4. **Ranked list with genre/decade filter chips** — minimal, one-pass filtering off the existing `year`/genre schema fields. *Acceptance:* no-match state shows a nudge, not a blank screen; filters never block or gate the base ranked list.

5. **TV-addable-but-ranking-disabled boundary** — TV titles can be added/logged for free but show a visibly disabled ranking action with a real "Unlock with FaveOff+" CTA. *Acceptance:* adding a TV title never blocks or alters movie ranking state; the disabled row is one clean, non-modal explainer, not a forced interruption.

6. **25-item free movie ranking cap** — enforced with its own non-blocking capped state. *Acceptance:* the cap state appears exactly at the 25th ranked movie; further title adds still succeed; only ranking beyond 25 is blocked.

7. **Taste Card generation + ShareLink** — `ImageRenderer` → branded poster-grid card → native share sheet. *Acceptance:* card is legible at iMessage-thumbnail size with a persistent brand mark; rendered as a static image so later title deletion can never corrupt an already-shared card.

8. **Real sandboxed StoreKit 2 purchase flow** — local `.storekit` config, both subscription products, real buy/pending/failed states, `Transaction.currentEntitlements` reconciliation on launch, visible Restore Purchases. *Acceptance:* a forced network failure or slow checkout stays fully contained to the purchase sheet and leaves the ranking loop completely unaffected — a real test case, not an assumption.

9. **Crash/interruption-safe persistence (including delete-safety)** — insertion state (`rankingState`, persisted `lo`/`hi`/`lastComparedIndex`/`skipBudget`) is always derived from SwiftData truth, never assumed in-memory. This must-have's acceptance criteria explicitly cover deletion, since it's the same failure category (the thing an in-flight insertion referenced is gone): deleting a title mid-active-comparison cancels that insertion cleanly, returns to the list with explicit "title removed, comparison canceled" copy, no crash, no dangling comparison references. *Acceptance:* kill the app mid-insertion, relaunch, and it resumes from persisted bounds exactly; delete a title while it's on-screen in a live comparison and the app recovers cleanly instead of crashing.

10. **Accessibility as a build gate on the Compare and Placed screens specifically** — measured contrast ≥4.5:1 body-weight / ≥3:1 large-bold text across the full Dynamic Type range, VoiceOver labels on every actionable element, 44pt touch targets. *Acceptance:* a Compare or Placed screen that hasn't been contrast-checked isn't considered a finished must-have — it's an incomplete one. This is folded directly into must-haves #2 and #3's definition of done, not a freestanding polish pass.

**Additional testable acceptance framing that applies across the whole must-have list:** count the taps between "user just added their 3rd movie" and "user is looking at their ranked list of 3" — if the TV-lock explainer, cap warning, or paywall CTA appears anywhere in that path unprompted (without the user tapping something that leads there), that's a failure. Policy/boundary screens are allowed to exist; they're not allowed to intrude on a path nobody asked to be shown.

**Should-have:** metadata enrichment via an optional protocol-backed provider (offline stub remains the default, never load-bearing); per-title "confidence" indicator showing how many real comparisons it's actually been through; delete-as-a-discoverable-UI-affordance (where the delete action actually lives — swipe, detail screen, etc. — as opposed to delete-safety itself, which is a Must); non-blocking "similar title already exists" duplicate nudge; light micro-haptics on comparison taps.

**Could-have:** brand-forward visual/motion polish (film-reel transitions) beyond the restrained baseline; filter-selection persistence across sessions; queue-depth indicator for rapid multi-add (explicitly deferred, separable from the crash-safety persistence which is already a Must); CSV/history import.

**Won't-build:** WidgetKit, tutorial/onboarding screens, manual drag-to-reorder, social graph/feed, periodic recap cards, saved/named sub-rank profiles, music/album ranking track, live metadata/poster API as a load-bearing dependency, push notifications, custom Photos-save button, data export/backup beyond the Taste Card image, classic Elo, full Ford-Johnson merge-insertion, full round-robin n² comparisons, forced re-comparisons.
phase_outputs.design_discussion:
Both sides actually landed in the same place this round. Codex came in and conceded both open points before Claude even had to push — dropping the separate Browse screen in favor of Claude's single Library spine (arguing it's not just cleaner, it's the faster, lower-risk ship shape and better protects the "no surprise policy screens" rule), and agreeing Compare should have no visible progress/confidence meter since it fights the "fun ritual, not a checklist" promise the whole app is built on.

Claude's round 2 message then builds on that agreement rather than reopening it: it folds Codex's "pending insertion" idea back in as a slim resume banner that only appears at the top of Library when there's a crash-recovered in-flight insertion waiting — not a permanent second view, just a narrow rescue path for the interruption case. It also catches something real buried in Codex's original wording: Codex had listed "left/right/tie/skip" as four options, and Claude points out "tie" is actually a new vote type that was never scoped and would break the locked sort-order invariant (binary insertion has no valid slot for "equal"). Claude's fix — treat tie and skip as the same button, just call it skip, matching all the copy already locked in earlier phases — isn't something Codex has explicitly signed off on in text, but it's a direct, uncontroversial correction of an inconsistency in Codex's own proposal, not a new fight, and nothing in the thread pushes back on it.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:**
- **Library** (root, single `NavigationStack`, no tab bar) — the one home screen: Movies|TV segmented control at top, filter chips (genre/decade) that only appear once there are enough titles to make filtering meaningful, the ranked list itself (poster, condensed rank number, title, year; TV rows shown dimmed with a lock glyph and "Unlock with FaveOff+" instead of a rank number), a toolbar "Add title" action, a toolbar Taste Card / share icon, and — only when a crash-recovered insertion is pending at launch — a slim top-of-list resume banner ("Finish placing [Title]") that jumps straight back into Compare at its persisted bounds. Non-blocking inline banner at the bottom of the list once the 25-item free cap is hit.
- **Add Title** — medium-detent form sheet: name (autofocused), Movie/TV type toggle, optional year/genre as visually secondary fields. Save returns to Library or chains directly into Compare.
- **Compare** — full-screen cover, total focus, no parent context visible. Two posters (or initials/color-block cards, same visual weight) with a VS divider; tap either to choose. One smaller, clearly secondary "Skip" affordance below the divider — this is the only alternate action; "tie" is not a separate option, it's the same control as skip, using the skip copy/logic already locked. No progress bar, no comparison counter, no confidence meter anywhere on this screen.
- **Placed** — full-screen cover, reached directly after a comparison resolves. Big rank reveal in the condensed display face, "ahead of X, behind Y" for normal placements, the locked cold-start and skip-fallback copy variants for the edge cases, one button ("Keep going") back to Library. This screen — not a live meter during Compare — is where the app is honest about how a title landed.
- **Taste Card** — large-detent sheet off the Library toolbar icon: live ImageRenderer preview of the poster-grid card, ShareLink below it.
- **Paywall** — sheet, the same single instance regardless of entry point (TV-lock row, cap banner, or toolbar upgrade button), with the full loading/pending/failed/purchased/restore state matrix.

**Primary user flow:** Cold start → Library empty state ("Add your first movie," button doubles as the empty-state CTA) → Add Title sheet → save → Compare (if insertion needed) or straight to Placed (0/1-item cold start, auto-placed) → Placed reward screen → "Keep going" back to Library, now showing the updated ranked list in place. TV adds always succeed and show as a locked row without ever touching movie ranking. Hitting the 25-item cap doesn't block adds, only further ranking, surfaced via the inline banner. Any purchase attempt lives entirely inside the Paywall sheet and never affects the ranking loop underneath it, success or failure. If the app was killed mid-insertion, relaunch shows the resume banner at the top of Library instead of silently dropping or auto-placing the title.

**State model per screen:**
- Library: `emptyNoItems`, `listWithItems` (with or without resume banner), `emptyFiltered` (nudge, not blank), `cappedBanner` visible once cap reached, standard `loading`/`error`.
- Add Title: `idle` → `saving` → `unranked`/`failed`.
- Compare: derives entirely from persisted `lo/hi/lastComparedIndex/skipBudget`; states are `pairReady`, `choicePending`, `skipPending`, `terminatedBySkips`; an interruption state (opposing title deleted mid-screen) cancels gracefully back to Library with a toast, no crash.
- Placed: `exactPlacement`, `fallbackPlacement` (honest skip-fallback copy), `cold-start` copy variants for 0/1-item lists — never a broken "#1 of 1, ahead of nothing" string.
- Paywall: `available`, `loadingPurchase`, `purchasePending`, `purchaseFailed` (dismiss always reachable), `purchased`, `restorePending`, `notEntitled`.

**Visual direction:** Dark warm charcoal base (not true black), marquee red reserved strictly for accents/actions. Condensed bold display face only for short strings (rank numbers, Placed title, section headers); standard Dynamic-Type system text everywhere else, especially anything VoiceOver reads or that can wrap. Poster-first card composition throughout. Film-reel-style wipe transition specifically on the Compare/Placed sequence, falling back to a plain crossfade under Reduce Motion.

**Accessibility notes:** Compare and Placed are hard gates — measured (not eyeballed) contrast ≥4.5:1 body-weight / ≥3:1 large-bold across the full Dynamic Type range before either screen is considered done. VoiceOver labels describe the actual choice ("Select [Title A] over [Title B]"), Skip gets its own explicit label, both poster tap targets stay ≥44×44pt even on the smallest phone width, and the single-sentence Placed announcement always includes rank plus both neighbors.

**Named open risk carried forward, not re-litigated:** betting Compare's own visual weight (posters need to read as obviously tappable) is enough to make the interaction self-evident with zero instructional copy anywhere — if build/test surfaces real first-time confusion, the fix is strengthening Compare's own affordances (bigger VS divider, more obvious tap zones), never a bolted-on tutorial screen.
phase_outputs.design_handoff:
CONSENSUS: YES

Here's what happened this round, and it's a cleaner convergence than it looks at first glance. Codex's round-2 message fully adopted Claude's warm-charcoal palette wholesale — background `#15100F`/`#1E1918`/`#272020`, border `#33292A`, accent `#E22C3C`, text `#F6F1ED`/`#AFA7A2` — down to the exact hex values, plus added a `textDisabled` token. Claude's round-2 message was written concurrently and doesn't know that happened yet — it's still arguing against Codex's *round-1* cooler palette (`#111217` etc.) using the "warm charcoal was already locked two phases ago" argument. But since Codex already landed on Claude's exact numbers, that fight is over before it started; there's no real daylight left on color.

On navigation, Codex explicitly took Claude's single-`ActiveInsertionFlow`-cover rule as a hard requirement — Compare and Placed are one `.fullScreenCover(item:)`, never two independent booleans — so that's locked without dispute. Component naming converged too: `PosterTile` wins over `PosterCard`, Codex's `DeleteAction` (swipe + detail-screen fallback) and skeleton-loading state both get folded in as agreed improvements.

The only place they're not saying identical words is Claude Design scope, and even that's more a difference of generosity than substance: Codex's actual resolution is icon-only, and only included the full screen-by-screen prompt as a "here it is if you still want it" fallback rather than a real ask. Claude's counter-offer — icon prompt plus one narrow, bounded 3-frame "mood check" (Library, Compare, Placed) generated strictly from the locked tokens/layout and explicitly forbidden from inventing new navigation or copy — doesn't contradict Codex's position, it's just a slightly larger version of the same "external design shouldn't become a second source of truth" stance Codex already agreed with. Nothing here needs another round.

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen specification**

- **Library** (root, single `NavigationStack`, no tab bar): Movies|TV segmented control at top to make the track boundary spatial; genre/decade `FilterChip` row appears only once there are enough titles to make filtering meaningful (suppressed below that threshold, not shown empty); ranked list of `LibraryRow`/`PosterTile` (poster, condensed rank badge, title, year), TV rows rendered as `LockedTVRow` (dimmed tile, lock glyph, "Unlock with FaveOff+" in place of rank — never blocks add or logging); toolbar "Add Title" and "Taste Card" actions; `InlineBanner` styled two ways — `resumeInsertion` (top of list, only when a crash-recovered in-flight insertion exists, "Finish placing [Title]" → jumps straight into the active flow at persisted bounds) and `capReached` (bottom of list, only at exactly 25 ranked movies, non-blocking). States: `loading` (row skeleton/shimmer), `emptyNoItems` ("Add your first movie" doubling as CTA), `list`, `listWithFilters`, `emptyFiltered` (nudge, not blank), `loadingFailure` (inline retry).

- **Add Title**: medium-detent form sheet. Title field autofocused, Movie/TV type segmented control directly under it, year/genre visually secondary and clearly optional. Save is instant/local-first, routes to the active insertion flow (Compare) or straight to `placed` for 0/1-item cold starts. States: `idle` → `saving` → `saved`/`failed` (typed content preserved on failure, inline retry, no dropped input).

- **Compare + Placed** (one continuous ritual, one `.fullScreenCover(item:)` bound to a single `ActiveInsertionFlow` value — never two independent covers, so there is no dismiss-flash back to Library between decision and placement): full-screen, zero parent chrome. Compare shows two `PosterTile`s (poster or deterministic initials/color-block, identical visual weight either way) with a VS `CompareDivider`, tap either to choose, one secondary `SkipButton` below — no progress bar, no comparison counter, no confidence meter anywhere on this screen; "tie" is not a separate action, it's Skip. If the opposing title is deleted mid-screen: cancel gracefully back to Library with a toast ("Title removed, comparison canceled"), no crash. Placed follows immediately inside the same cover: rank reveal in condensed display type, `#N, ahead of X, behind Y` for normal placements, the locked cold-start copy ("First one in — add another to build your ranking") and skip-fallback copy ("Added near the bottom for now — compare it later to refine it") for edge cases, one button ("Keep going") back to Library. Flow states: `comparing`, `processingChoice`, `skip`, `terminatedBySkips`, `cancelledByInterruption`, `placed` (variants: `exact`, `fallback`, `coldStart`).

- **Taste Card**: large-detent sheet off the Library toolbar. Enforces a `resolvingArt` loading state before the live preview — the render never fires on partially-loaded poster art, since `ImageRenderer` snapshots synchronously and would otherwise silently capture blank tiles; trivially satisfied today since default art is synchronous initials/color-block, but the gate stays in place so it doesn't regress when optional metadata enrichment is wired in later. States: `resolvingArt`, `ready` (live preview + `ShareLink`), `renderFailed` (retry), `sharing`.

- **Paywall**: single shared sheet instance regardless of entry point (TV-lock row, cap banner, toolbar upgrade). States: `available`, `loadingProducts`, `purchasePending`, `purchaseFailed` (dismiss always reachable), `restored`, `entitled`, `dismissed`. Ranking loop is fully unaffected by any state here, success or failure.

**Design tokens**
- `backgroundCanvas` `#15100F`, `backgroundSurface` `#1E1918`, `backgroundElevated` `#272020`, `borderLine` `#33292A` (hairline borders instead of shadows — near-black fields don't render shadows well)
- `accentRed` `#E22C3C` — action fills and short large/bold display strings only, never body text (re-verify large-bold ≥3:1 against `backgroundCanvas` before final)
- `textPrimary` `#F6F1ED`, `textSecondary` `#AFA7A2` (both independently confirmed ≥4.5:1 body-weight against the canvas), `textDisabled` `#6F6660`
- Type: system font throughout for Dynamic Type integrity; condensed width (`Font.system(...).width(.condensed)`) reserved strictly for short non-wrapping strings — rank numbers, Placed hero title, section headers, chip labels. No embedded custom font file.
- Spacing: 8pt base grid (4/8/12/16/24/32/48). Radius: 16pt cards, 14pt buttons, fully-rounded chips.
- Motion: standard state changes 0.25s ease-in-out; the one cinematic beat is the Compare→Placed internal wipe inside the shared cover, ~0.35s horizontal masked transition with a faint sprocket-edge detail, collapsing to a flat 0.2s crossfade under Reduce Motion — no partial-motion exceptions.

**Component inventory**: `NavigationShell`, `LibraryRow`/`PosterTile`, `RankBadge`, `LockedTVRow`, `TrackSegmentedControl`, `FilterChip`, `InlineBanner` (resume + cap variants), `EmptyState`, `ErrorState`, `AddTitleFieldRow`, `CompareDivider`, `SkipButton`, `PlacedReveal`, `TasteCardGrid`, `PaywallProductTile`, `PrimaryButton`/`SecondaryButton`, `DeleteAction` (swipe + detail-screen fallback), `SectionSpacer`.

**Navigation/state architecture (binding rule)**: Library owns exactly one `activeSheet: SheetKind?` (Add Title / Taste Card / Paywall, each `.sheet(item:)`) and one `activeFlow: ActiveInsertionFlow?` (Compare/Placed, `.fullScreenCover(item:)`) — never competing presentations. The resume banner constructs the identical `ActiveInsertionFlow` value from persisted `lo`/`hi`/`lastComparedIndex`/`skipBudget`, so there's exactly one code path into the ritual whether triggered by a fresh Add or a crash-recovery resume. All list-driving views read through the repository/view-model boundary — no view that also does navigation touches `@Query` directly.

**Accessibility**: Compare and Placed are hard gates — measured ≥4.5:1 body / ≥3:1 large-bold contrast across the full Dynamic Type range before either is done. VoiceOver labels state the actual choice ("Select [Title A] over [Title B]"), Skip gets its own explicit label, 44×44pt minimum on every tap target including full poster bounds, no color-only status cues (lock/cap states pair a glyph with text), and the Placed VO announcement is one sentence covering rank plus both neighbors.

**Claude Design decision**: No external design pass for the app's screens — the token system, component inventory, and state model above are specific enough for direct SwiftUI implementation, and handing the full UI to an external pass risks producing frames that contradict the locked interaction contract (the single-cover invariant, the "no unprompted policy screens" rule, the honest placement copy variants) since an external tool has no visibility into that state machine. Two narrow, bounded external prompts are approved instead:
1. **App icon**: "Design an app icon for FaveOff, a cinematic movie/TV ranking app — near-black warm charcoal base (#15100F), marquee red accent (#E22C3C), bold condensed numeral or ticket/film-reel motif, award-show/red-carpet feeling, must read clearly at 60×60pt down to the smallest home-screen size, no text."
2. **Optional 3-frame mood check** (Library with a few ranked rows, Compare mid-decision, Placed reward state) — generated strictly from the token values and layout rules above, explicitly framed as a visual sanity check only, not a spec, and explicitly instructed not to invent new navigation, copy, or states.
phase_outputs.ios_architecture_review:
CONSENSUS: YES

Both of them are pulling in the exact same direction here — Codex laid out the service-oriented skeleton (repository façade, feature services, StoreKit isolation, zero third-party packages, pure ranking engine) and Claude's message doesn't push back on any of it; it tightens screws Codex left loose and adds a few things Codex's draft simply hadn't gotten to yet. Nothing in either message contradicts the other.

Where they agree outright: SwiftUI + SwiftData + StoreKit 2 only, no third-party dependencies at all, no ML/AR/Vision frameworks (both give the same "unnecessary failure surface" reasoning), the ranking engine must be a pure, UI/persistence-free unit (Codex frames it as "no UI," Claude sharpens it to "zero SwiftData imports, zero ModelContext" — same rule, stated more strictly), metadata lookup stays behind a protocol with an offline default and remote lookup as pure background enrichment, StoreKit purchase state must be fully isolated so failures can't touch the ranking loop, and CloudKit/widget entitlements stay explicitly out of scope for this build.

What Claude adds without contradicting Codex: the CloudKit-migration trap (SwiftData + CloudKit needs optional relationships, no unique constraints, default values on non-optional fields — so lock a `VersionedSchema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan` now even with one version, since the alternative is a destructive migration on the first real update), a concrete fix for the delete-during-comparison race (re-fetch by persistent ID right before acting, nil means `cancelledByInterruption`, and it should be a real XCTest not just a described scenario), the UI-visible consequence of Codex's own atomicity risk (bulk `position` rewrites reading as 15 rows glitching unless the bulk shift is unanimated and only the Placed reveal animates), the `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` manifest requirement (a real App Store submission blocker Codex hadn't named), the requirement that the `Transaction.currentEntitlements` listener run as a persistent launch-time `Task` rather than a one-shot call, and a concrete unit test that runs the actual WCAG contrast formula against the locked design tokens instead of leaving "measured, not eyeballed" as an unverified claim.

The one open thread that isn't a disagreement, just unfinished business: Codex proposed a specific three-entity schema (`Title`, `Comparison`, a dedicated `InsertionContext`) and Claude's message doesn't explicitly confirm or dispute that shape — it's silent on it rather than contesting it. Since nothing in Claude's CloudKit-compatibility point conflicts with Codex's entity split (if anything, a separate `InsertionContext` entity makes the "every relationship optional" CloudKit constraint easier to satisfy than cramming insertion bounds directly onto `Title`), I'm treating this as compatible-by-default rather than a real fight.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture:** Service-oriented layering — `App` shell owns scene/session state; `@Observable` view models sit between every view and a `TitleRepository` protocol (no view touches SwiftData directly, matching the navigation binding rule already locked in `design_handoff`); feature services for ranking, metadata lookup, purchase, and Taste Card rendering. The `RankingEngine` (bounded binary insertion, skip/2-skip-termination, `lo/hi/lastComparedIndex/skipBudget` state machine) is a pure Swift type — zero SwiftData imports, zero `ModelContext` dependency, plain arrays and IDs in and out — wrapped by an `InsertionCoordinator` use case that persists state after every single step so kill/relaunch always resumes from durable truth, never in-memory view state.

**Apple frameworks:** SwiftUI, SwiftData, StoreKit 2, `ImageRenderer` + `ShareLink`, SF Symbols, XCTest/Swift Testing, XCUITest. Explicitly no Vision, ARKit, Core ML, or any ML/AR framework — the spec already named the ranking engine, not ML, as the one hard technical capability, and none of these frameworks earn their failure-surface cost (permission prompts, model load latency, device-capability branching) for zero locked user value.

**Persistence and local data plan:** SwiftData as sole source of truth, accessed only through the repository façade. Entities: `Title` (user-entered content: name, type, optional year/genre, deterministic poster color/text seed, track, rank position, `rankingState`), `Comparison` (decision audit trail: winner/loser IDs, track, timestamp, skip flag, flow ID), and a dedicated `InsertionContext` entity holding the active insertion's `lo`/`hi`/`lastComparedIndex`/`skipBudget` rather than overloading `Title` with half a state machine — cleaner to reason about and easier to keep every relationship optional the way CloudKit compatibility requires later. Lock a `VersionedSchema` + `SchemaMigrationPlan` from day one, even with a single schema version, so the eventual CloudKit sync roadmap doesn't force a destructive migration on first update. Any remote poster art is cached as files with a pointer ID on `Title`, never as `Data` blobs in SwiftData rows. All `position` writes for a given insertion happen as one atomic service-level operation with list animation disabled on the bulk shift — the user should never see 15 rows visibly reshuffle; only the Placed reveal animates. Compare re-fetches its two titles by persistent ID immediately before acting rather than holding long-lived references, so a mid-screen deletion resolves to a clean `cancelledByInterruption` state instead of a silent no-op or crash.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** Zero runtime permission prompts anywhere in the app — no camera, location, contacts, microphone, notifications, or photo-library access (ShareLink's built-in save covers export). Network is used only for optional metadata/poster enrichment, strictly opt-in and never load-bearing for add/compare/rank. No analytics, no device fingerprinting, no identifiers beyond internal UUIDs. A `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` manifest declaring required-reason API usage must be in the project from the first build, not added before archiving — this is submission-blocking, not polish. StoreKit needs the In-App Purchase capability on the target; nothing else needs an entitlement. CloudKit container/entitlement setup and WidgetKit's App Group plumbing both stay explicitly out of scope for this build.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages, full stop. Everything the app needs — SwiftData, StoreKit 2, ImageRenderer, ShareLink, SF Symbols, `AsyncImage` if the optional provider is ever wired in — is first-party, which trivially satisfies the permissive-license policy and removes an entire category of maintenance/license risk from an app that has no functional need for any external package. A future proposal to add one (e.g., for image caching) should be treated as a rule violation to push back on by default.

**Testing implications:** Because the ranking engine is pure, its correctness (bounded-insertion ordering, transitivity invariants, deterministic 2-skip termination and fallback placement, duplicate-title handling) is unit-testable in milliseconds with no `ModelContainer`. Persistence/integration tests cover crash-recovery resume from stale `InsertionContext` bounds, delete-during-comparison resolving to `cancelledByInterruption` (constructed explicitly: start an insertion, delete the opposing title from a second context, assert no crash and no corrupted bounds), 0/1-item cold-start placement copy, TV-rank-lock behavior, and the 25-item cap (adds still succeed, only ranking blocks). A dedicated unit test runs the real WCAG relative-luminance/contrast formula against the locked token pairs (`textPrimary`/`textSecondary` vs. `backgroundCanvas`, `accentRed` large-bold vs. `backgroundCanvas`) and asserts the 4.5:1/3:1 thresholds numerically as part of the normal test target — "measured, not eyeballed" gets an actual test, not just a claim. XCUITest covers the one true end-to-end path (add → compare → placed → browse → share) plus resume-banner recovery and paywall isolation under a forced purchase failure, using deliberate accessibility identifiers rather than label-text matching. The `Transaction.currentEntitlements` listener runs as a persistent `Task` attached at launch (not a one-shot call tied to a button tap), since entitlement changes can complete while backgrounded or across a relaunch.
phase_outputs.tech_specs:
Round 2 actually closed every real gap from round 1 — this isn't a "let's call it good enough" convergence, both sides explicitly walked through each open item and landed on the same answer.

**What got resolved, point by point:** Codex conceded outright on `lastComparedTitleID: UUID?` over `lastComparedIndex: Int`, using Claude's own "re-fetch by persistent ID, never trust an index" logic against the earlier draft. Codex also dropped the `FilmTitle`/`StoredTitle` domain-vs-persistence split and switched to using the `Title` `@Model` directly through the repository — both sides' round-2 contracts now show the same single-type shape. `RankingEngine` converged on the static-func `enum` (not a protocol) in both drafts, with Claude's added reasoning (an enum with no cases physically cannot hold state, so purity is compiler-enforced, not just a convention) going unchallenged. Codex's `RankingTransition`/`SkipFallbackReason` combo — which let a "deleted opponent" interruption be encoded as a placement reason on the same struct as an actual rank position — got replaced with Claude's cleaner split (`InsertionStepResult` for placement outcomes, `InterruptionReason` as a fully separate path for cancellation); Codex's own round-2 contract adopted that shape. View models are `@Observable` in both, not `ObservableObject`. `PurchaseService`, `MetadataLookupService`, and `TasteCardRenderer` (returning `Image`, not `Data`) all landed on matching signatures. `InsertionFlowViewModel` beat `CompareFlowViewModel` — Codex just used Claude's name. The single-local-package proposal for `RankingEngine` (`FaveOffRanking`, Foundation-only) went unchallenged in round 2, so that's settled by default.

What's left is genuinely trivial — a couple of method names that mean the same thing (`fetchByID` vs `title(id:)`, `removeTitle` vs `delete`), a couple of small additive types one side proposed and the other didn't get to yet (`AccessibilityIdentifiers` for XCUITest targeting, `SwiftDataContainerFactory` as a test seam, a `capReached` repository check, a fixture helper) — nothing here needs a round 3, I'm just picking one name for each and folding the useful additions in below.

One thing I'm fixing rather than just merging: Codex's round-2 `Title` added `@Attribute(.unique) var id: UUID`. That directly contradicts the CloudKit-compatibility rule locked back in `ios_architecture_review` — SwiftData+CloudKit forbids unique constraints on attributes, and the entire reason we locked a `VersionedSchema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan` this early was to avoid a destructive migration when CloudKit sync gets added later. `UUID()` generation already gives us practical uniqueness without a schema-level constraint, so I'm dropping `@Attribute(.unique)` from the final contract.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** Service-oriented, single app target plus one local Swift package. `FaveOffApp` composes an `AppEnvironment` once at launch (repositories, `InsertionCoordinator`, metadata/purchase/card services) and injects it via `.environment`; no view or view model constructs its own `ModelContext` or `URLSession`. Every view model is `@Observable @MainActor` and talks only to repository protocols — never `@Query` directly in a view that also does navigation. The one hard technical capability, `RankingEngine`, is a static-function `enum` with zero SwiftData/SwiftUI imports, living in a standalone local package (`FaveOffRanking`, Foundation-only) so a stray `import SwiftData` there is a compile error, not a review catch. `InsertionCoordinator` is the sole bridge between that pure engine and persistence: it persists `InsertionContext` after every single step, re-fetches both compared titles by persistent ID immediately before acting on a choice/skip, and resolves a missing title to `cancelledByInterruption` rather than a crash or silent no-op. Library owns exactly one `activeSheet: SheetKind?` and one `activeFlow: ActiveInsertionFlow?`; Compare and Placed are one `.fullScreenCover(item:)`, never two.

**File/module layout:** One app target with disciplined folders (not compiled module walls, since the only invariant worth a hard boundary is engine purity, and that's already covered by the package): `FaveOff/App` (shell, `AppEnvironment` composition root), `FaveOff/Models` (the three `@Model` types, `FaveOffSchemaV1`, `FaveOffMigrationPlan`), `FaveOff/Repositories` (the only files importing SwiftData directly), `FaveOff/Services` (`InsertionCoordinator`, metadata/purchase/card-render services, `ContrastValidator`), `FaveOff/ViewModels`, `FaveOff/Views` (mirrors the design-handoff screen inventory), `FaveOff/Navigation` (the two presentation enums). `Packages/FaveOffRanking` is the one local package: `RankingEngine`, `InsertionState`, `ComparisonPair`, `InsertionStepResult`, `PlacementKind` — Foundation-only, no app-target imports. Tests: `FaveOffRankingTests` needs no `ModelContainer` at all; `FaveOffTests` covers repositories/integration with an in-memory container; `FaveOffUITests` covers the one true end-to-end path using the `AccessibilityIdentifiers` below.

**Data models:** `Title`, `Comparison`, `InsertionContext` as `@Model` classes, no `@Relationship` properties anywhere — every cross-entity link is a plain `UUID` resolved through a repository, which is what keeps deletion safe (a deleted title just makes a lookup return `nil`, no cascade rule to reason about) and keeps every relationship CloudKit-optional by construction. No unique-attribute constraints, for the same CloudKit-migration-safety reason. `Genre` and `MediaType` are fixed enums, never free text. `InsertionContext.lastComparedTitleID` is a `UUID?`, never an array index, because indices drift the moment another insertion or a delete shifts `position` values underneath a backgrounded flow.

**Persistence strategy:** SwiftData is the sole source of truth, reached only through `TitleRepository`/`ComparisonRepository`/`InsertionRepository`. `FaveOffSchemaV1` + an (initially empty-stage) `FaveOffMigrationPlan` are locked from the first commit. `applyPositions` is one atomic write per insertion with the bulk shift unanimated — only the Placed reveal animates, so the user never sees rows visibly reshuffle. `SwiftDataContainerFactory` is the injectable seam that makes real-app vs. in-memory-test containers a one-line swap.

**Testing strategy:** Pure-engine suite (bounded-insertion ordering, transitivity, two-skip deterministic termination and fallback placement, 0/1-item cold start, duplicate titles) runs in milliseconds against `FaveOffRanking` alone. `ContrastValidator` gets a numeric unit test asserting the real WCAG thresholds against every locked token pair. Integration tests (in-memory `ModelContainer`) cover crash-recovery resume from a stale `InsertionContext`, delete-during-comparison resolving to `cancelledByInterruption`, cap boundary (26th add still succeeds, only ranking blocks), and TV-rank-lock behavior. One `XCUITest` walks add → compare → placed → browse → share plus resume-banner recovery and paywall isolation under a forced purchase failure, targeting the `AccessibilityIdentifiers` below rather than label text.

This one's done — Codex made the case for the milestone split, Claude actually changed his mind (not just conceded) because he recognized a milestone boundary is a real enforced gate downstream while his own round-3 proposal was just an internal convention someone could skip under pressure. The two small tightenings Claude asked for on the way out — Monetization Surfaces' exit criteria explicitly re-running Core Ritual's tap-count test unmodified, and Hardening's exit criteria explicitly re-running the full existing test suite rather than just walking the happy path by hand — are both natural, uncontested extensions of things Codex already said in the same message (Codex's own round 4 text already says "we re-run that exact test to prove no regression" for the first one). Nothing left unresolved.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Milestones (relative-sized, dependency-ordered, not calendar-dated):**

1. **Bootstrapping (S)** — Real wiring, not compile-only. Exit criteria: (a) an app-target view model calls a public `FaveOffRanking` API (e.g. `RankingEngine.startInsertion`) across the package boundary and gets a correct `InsertionState` back — proves the package's public surface actually works from where it's used, not just that its own test target sees internal symbols; (b) a throwaway test against the real simulator StoreKit testing environment loads both product IDs via the local `.storekit` config and drives one full purchase through to an observed transition in `Transaction.currentEntitlements`; (c) `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` exists in the project. If either (a) or (b) fails, stop and fix here — this is the cheapest point in the whole build to discover that class of flakiness.

2. **Engine (S)** — `FaveOffRanking` as a Foundation-only static enum, zero SwiftData/SwiftUI imports. Tests: bounded-insertion ordering, transitivity, deterministic two-skip termination and fallback placement (`testTwoSkipFallbackBottomPlacement`), 0/1-item cold start, duplicate-ID stability.

3. **Models + Container (S/M, parallel with Engine)** — `Title`, `Comparison`, `InsertionContext`, `FaveOffSchemaV1` + migration plan, `SwiftDataContainerFactory` (real disk + in-memory), repositories. No UI dependency. Exit: container opens both modes, schema bootstraps, CRUD + rank-position writes work.

4. **Coordinator (M, hard-gated)** — `InsertionCoordinator` bridging the pure engine to persistence: re-fetches both comparison participants by UUID immediately before acting, recomputes full ranked-ID list by position every step (explicit O(n) strategy, no pagination, locked to prevent someone "optimizing" this into a source of drift bugs), persists `InsertionContext` after every step. Milestone is not done until these named tests exist and pass: `InsertionCoordinatorTests.testResumeAfterKillMidInsertion`, `InsertionCoordinatorTests.testDeleteOpponentDuringCompareCancelsCleanly`, a stale-context reconciliation test, and `testTwoSkipFallbackBottomPlacement` at the coordinator-integration level. Nothing downstream starts until these are green.

5. **Core Ritual (M/L)** — Library, Add Title, Compare, Placed as one shared full-screen flow (`ActiveInsertionFlow`, single-cover invariant), full empty/loading/success/error states, toast recovery on interruption. Zero policy UI in this milestone — TV titles can be added but show no lock-state UI yet. Exit criteria includes a named, committed tap-count test: 0→3 movies through add→compare→placed→back-to-list with zero unprompted policy screens. This test is the baseline everything after it must not break.

6. **List Intelligence (S)** — Genre/decade filter chips, no-match nudge state. Additive only; filtering never blocks or gates the base ranked list. Depends on Core Ritual being stable.

7. **Taste Card (M)** — Card renderer with `resolvingArt` gate, tested explicitly against a deliberately slow fake metadata provider to prove `ImageRenderer` never snapshots a partially-loaded grid. `ShareLink` export verified under airplane mode.

8. **StoreKit (M/L)** — Full purchase/restore/entitlement state machine, persistent launch-time entitlement listener, paywall failure fully isolated from ranking state. Dual verification locked as the real pass condition: automated `Transaction.currentEntitlements`/restore/purchase-state transition tests are the acceptance bar; manual sandbox click-through is a smoke test only, not the primary verification method. This is the explicit, in-writing fallback for StoreKit sandbox flakiness — separate from and in addition to the architectural isolation guarantee.

9. **Monetization Surfaces (S)** — TV-lock row, 25-item movie cap banner, upgrade CTAs wired into the real Library UI using the already-tested paywall. Exit criteria explicitly includes re-running Core Ritual's tap-count test unmodified and green, plus new tests confirming the lock/cap rows themselves behave correctly (TV add never blocks movie ranking, cap fires exactly at 25, adds still succeed past cap). This re-run is what makes the milestone split a real regression guard rather than just a sequencing preference.

10. **A11y + Contrast Gate (M)** — `ContrastValidator`'s numeric WCAG checks run continuously as design tokens land (not deferred), plus one final hard gate before Hardening: Compare and Placed must measure ≥4.5:1 body / ≥3:1 large-bold across the full Dynamic Type range, every actionable control has a VoiceOver label and a 44pt target, no color-only status cues.

11. **Hardening + Release Prep (M)** — Full end-to-end XCUITest (add→compare→placed→browse→share, resume-banner recovery, forced purchase failure with ranking unaffected). Exit criteria explicitly requires the *entire* existing test suite — engine, coordinator, contrast, taste-card resolvingArt, and the Monetization Surfaces regression test — re-run and green, not just a hand-walked happy path; this catches the case where later work touches shared code (repository methods, `Title` fields, navigation state) and silently breaks an assertion locked several milestones earlier. Final deliverables: privacy manifest confirmed, clean-build verification, phase handoff artifact.

**Dependencies:** Engine and Models+Container have no dependency on each other and run in parallel; both must complete before Coordinator. Coordinator must be fully green (its four named tests) before Core Ritual begins. Core Ritual's tap-count test must be committed and green before List Intelligence, Taste Card, or Monetization Surfaces work starts. Taste Card depends on a stable ranked-list model. StoreKit depends on the composition root and repositories being stable. Monetization Surfaces depends on both Core Ritual (for the baseline test) and StoreKit (for the real paywall it wires in). A11y contrast checks run continuously from whenever tokens exist, with its final gate immediately preceding Hardening. Hardening is last and depends on everything.

**Risk mitigations:**
- Comparison/crash-recovery correctness → named, milestone-gating `InsertionCoordinator` tests, not a deferred QA pass.
- Setup/integration friction (package linking, StoreKit scheme config) → a real round-trip spike in Bootstrapping, before any feature work, so flakiness surfaces with maximum runway to fix it.
- Policy-screen regression corrupting the "no surprise interruption" promise → structural milestone boundary (Core Ritual before Monetization Surfaces) with an explicit non-regression re-run, not just an internal convention.
- StoreKit sandbox flakiness eating schedule → automated `Transaction`-API verification as the real pass bar, manual sandbox reduced to a smoke test — a second, independent fallback from the architectural isolation guarantee.
- Trust collapse from arbitrary placements → two-skip deterministic termination plus honest, tested placement copy.
- Accessibility/contrast drift during implementation → continuous checks plus a final hard numeric gate.
- Late-stage silent regressions from shared-code changes → full suite re-run required at Hardening, not just an E2E walkthrough.
- Schedule pressure → cut order is: widget (already out of scope this build) first, then design polish — never ranking engine, comparison loop, offline behavior, share flow, or StoreKit verification rigor.

**Verification plan:** Millisecond-fast pure-engine tests requiring no `ModelContainer`; numeric `ContrastValidator` tests against locked token pairs; in-memory-`ModelContainer` integration tests for crash-recovery, delete-during-comparison, cap boundary, and TV-lock behavior; a slow-fake-provider test proving the Taste Card's `resolvingArt` gate is real; one XCUITest walking the full add→compare→placed→browse→share path plus resume-banner recovery and paywall-isolation-under-failure; a committed regression test proving Monetization Surfaces doesn't reintroduce unprompted policy screens on the core path; and a full-suite green re-run as the literal exit condition for Hardening.

## Final Output

### Readiness verdict
**Conditionally ready — proceed to build with the fixes below folded in before the affected tasks are picked up.** Nothing here requires re-opening design, architecture, or the task graph. T-001 through T-008 (scaffold, engine, models, repositories) can start immediately with zero changes. T-006, T-009, T-015, and the paywall copy source-of-truth need one explicit addition each before they're built.

### Cross-phase consistency check
One real contradiction found, everything else in the seven prior phases is internally consistent:
- **FaveOff+ entitlement scope conflict**: `per_app_product_brief` describes four paid unlocks (TV ranking, uncapped movie ranking, saved/named sub-rank views, periodic recap cards). `app_features`'s locked Won't-build list cuts two of those four (saved sub-rank profiles, recap cards) without ever stating that as a deliberate downgrade against the product brief. No phase after `app_features` reconciled this back against the brief.

### Build blockers and fixes
1. **Paywall scope ambiguity (blocker)** — Fix: FaveOff+'s v1 entitlement surface is exactly two things: the TV ranking track, and removal of the 25-item movie cap. T-015's `PaywallView` copy and `PaywallProductTile` must be written from this two-item list, not the product brief's four-item pitch. Saved sub-rank profiles and recap cards are formally downgraded out of v1 here (ratifying, not silently inheriting, the `app_features` Won't-build list).
2. **No affordance for gate-lift on already-added, never-ranked titles (blocker)** — Fix: add to T-015's acceptance criteria — when a title is in an addable-but-unranked state (TV pre-unlock, or movies added past the 25 cap) and its gate lifts, tapping that row constructs the same `ActiveInsertionFlow` used by Add Title and the resume banner. Without this, a paying user opens their TV list post-purchase and sees unranked rows with no path forward, which directly undercuts the app's core trust promise at the exact moment they paid.
3. **No locked deployment target** — Fix: T-001 states iOS 17.0 as the minimum deployment target (required by the already-locked `@Observable` usage throughout `tech_specs`).
4. **`Genre` enum has no case list** — Fix: T-007 ships with a starter set: Action, Comedy, Drama, Horror, Thriller, Sci-Fi, Fantasy, Romance, Documentary, Animation, Mystery, Other.
5. **Track isolation not a named test** — Fix: add to T-009's acceptance criteria — the comparison candidate pool is scoped to the same `MediaType`; a movie is never paired against a TV title. This is implied by the two-track design everywhere else but was never a testable, named criterion.
6. **Missing accessibility identifiers for elements Hardening is required to test** — Fix: T-006's `AccessibilityIdentifiers` enum gets three more cases (resume banner, cap banner, TV-lock row) before it's called done, since T-017's E2E test is already required to exercise "resume-banner recovery" and can't reliably assert on it without a stable identifier.

### Final scope for build
Everything locked across `prompt_contract` through `task_assignments` stands as-is, with the six fixes above folded into T-001, T-006, T-007, T-009, and T-015 before those tasks are picked up. Explicitly ratified downgrades for this build: WidgetKit is out of v1 (already named as first-to-cut, no change), and — newly stated plainly here — periodic recap cards and saved/named sub-rank profiles are out of v1 despite being named in the product brief; FaveOff+ ships with exactly two entitlements (TV ranking track, uncapped movie ranking).

### Acceptance criteria checklist
- [ ] T-001 states iOS 17.0 deployment target
- [ ] T-006's `AccessibilityIdentifiers` includes resume banner, cap banner, TV-lock row
- [ ] T-007's `Genre` enum ships with the twelve-case starter set
- [ ] T-009's acceptance criteria include same-track-only comparison pairing
- [ ] T-015's `PaywallView` copy reflects exactly two entitlements (TV ranking, uncapped movies) — no mention of saved sub-rank views or recap cards
- [ ] T-015's acceptance criteria include: gate-lift on a previously-added, never-ranked title routes into the same `ActiveInsertionFlow` as Add Title / resume banner
- [ ] Every major workflow (add, compare, placed, browse/filter, share, TV-lock, cap, purchase, accessibility) has a named, testable acceptance criterion — confirmed true across the existing T-001–T-017 list
- [ ] Testing responsibilities are explicit per task — confirmed true, each task carries its own named tests as its definition of done
- [ ] Build can start without inventing product/design/architecture decisions — true once the six fixes above are applied

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

This is FaveOff — a local-first, dark-mode movie/TV ranking app where you build your real top 10 by picking one head-to-head match-up at a time instead of star ratings, then share it as a poster-grid Taste Card. Nine rounds of build_coordination went into it, almost all of it Claude working solo since Codex never got a working CLI turn across the whole phase — so what exists is a complete, thoughtfully-hardened app built and reviewed by one very persistent pair of eyes across nine passes, plus my own integration work wiring and re-verifying every round.

**What's actually in the app:** add a movie or TV title in seconds with deterministic offline placeholder art (no network dependency for the core loop) → get placed via a bounded binary-insertion comparison engine (never more than ⌈log₂(n+1)⌉ comparisons, with a "too close to call" skip that terminates deterministically after two in a row) → see a real Placed reveal screen with honest, non-generic copy for cold-starts and skip-fallbacks → browse your ranked list with genre/decade filter chips → generate and share a poster-grid Taste Card via `ImageRenderer` + `ShareLink`. Movies rank free up to 25; TV titles can be added and logged for free but stay ranking-locked until FaveOff+ ($3.99/mo or $24.99/yr), sold through a real StoreKit 2 flow (products, purchase, restore, a persistent launch-time entitlement listener) against a local `.storekit` sandbox config.

**What went into making it trustworthy, not just functional:** a from-scratch SwiftData persistence layer and ranking coordinator that didn't exist anywhere until I built it in round 1 (data_domain/services_utilities never got a live turn); a real monetization bug caught and fixed in round 2 (entitled users were stuck behind the movie cap, and a stale row could rank a locked title for free — both closed, with a second related bug caught and fixed in round 6); a hand-verified WCAG contrast validator in round 3 with real passing math, not eyeballed hex codes; a Dynamic-Type overflow and 44pt touch-target sweep across every screen in round 5; real test coverage for the Taste Card's trickiest state (the `resolvingArt` snapshot-race gate) in round 7; and, most importantly, a genuinely dead `swipeActions` delete affordance caught in round 8 — a bug that seven prior rounds of careful reading had all missed, because it's the exact class of thing that only shows up when something actually runs. This round closed out with a documented SwiftUI focus-timing fix on the Add Title sheet and a rigorous double-check that every `@State`+`@Observable` binding pattern in the app is on solid ground.

**The one honest limitation, unchanged across all nine rounds:** nobody — not me, not Claude, ever — got `xcodebuild` to actually run in this sandbox; every attempt stalled on an approval that never arrived non-interactively. Everything here has been verified by exhaustive line-by-line reading, hand-traced brace/reference-count checks on the Xcode project file (I re-verified the ID bookkeeping every single round a file was added), and in round 8, a genuine catch that reading alone can miss real bugs — which is itself the best evidence for what to do next.

**How to run it:** open `FaveOff.xcodeproj` in Xcode 16+ (iOS 17.0 deployment target), select the FaveOff scheme (it's already wired to `FaveOff/Configuration.storekit` for sandbox purchase testing), and build/run on a simulator or device. Run the `FaveOffTests` target to execute the existing Swift Testing suite (ranking engine bounds, monetization gating, contrast thresholds, Taste Card rendering states, filter behavior).

**What a next session should do first, in order:** (1) an actual clean build — given round 8's find, treat this as likely to surface at least one more real issue; (2) write the real-`InsertionCoordinator` crash/interruption tests (they still only exist against the UI lane's fake); (3) verify VoiceOver announcements with a real accessibility inspector pass, since contrast and touch-targets are now hand-verified but VoiceOver behavior isn't; (4) an end-to-end XCUITest and full-suite regression run. None of that blocks calling this iteration's build complete — the full locked v1 scope is genuinely built, wired, and as carefully reviewed as reading can make it.
